topic: Transformer architecture improvements in large language models 2020-2024
final_review:
The field of large language models has witnessed significant advancements in recent years, with a focus on improving the Transformer architecture (Gao et al., 2023). One area of research has been the use of retrieval-augmented generation, which has been surveyed by Gao et al. (2023) and shown to be effective in improving the performance of large language models.

In addition to these architectural improvements, there has been a growing interest in applying large language models to specific domains, such as chemistry (Bran et al., 2024) and medicine (Dave Van Veen et al., 2024; Hanwen Xu et al., 2024). For example, Bran et al. (2024) have demonstrated the potential of augmenting large language models with chemistry tools, while Dave Van Veen et al. (2024) have shown that adapted large language models can outperform medical experts in clinical text summarization. Similarly, Hanwen Xu et al. (2024) have developed a whole-slide foundation model for digital pathology from real-world data.

Explainability has also become an important area of research in large language models, with surveys by Zhao et al. (2024) and Yang et al. (2024) highlighting the need for more transparent and interpretable models. Furthermore, the use of large language models in autonomous agents has been explored, and there is a general consensus that large language models have the potential to revolutionize a wide range of fields, from chemistry to medicine (Bran et al., 2024; Dave Van Veen et al., 2024; Hanwen Xu et al., 2024). However, there are also concerns about the lack of explainability and transparency in these models (Zhao et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2024). Overall, the field of large language models is rapidly evolving, and further research is needed to fully realize the potential of these models.

In terms of current research, large language models are being applied to various domains, and their performance is being evaluated. The surveys by Zhao et al. (2024) and Yang et al. (2024) provide a comprehensive overview of the current state of large language models and their applications. The studies by Bran et al. (2024), Dave Van Veen et al. (2024), and Hanwen Xu et al. (2024) demonstrate the potential of large language models in chemistry and medicine.

References:
Bran, A. M., Cox, S., & Schilter, O. (2024). Augmenting large language models with chemistry tools. https://doi.org/10.1038/s42256-024-00832-8
Dave Van Veen, C. V. U., & Blankemeier, L. (2024). Adapted large language models can outperform medical experts in clinical text summarization. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-024-02855-5
Gao, Y., Xiong, Y., & Gao, X. (2023). Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models: A Survey. https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2312.10997
Hanwen Xu, N. U., & Bagga, J. (2024). A whole-slide foundation model for digital pathology from real-world data. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-024-07441-w
Yang, J., Jin, H., & Tang, R. (2024). Harnessing the Power of LLMs in Practice: A Survey on ChatGPT and Beyond. https://doi.org/10.1145/3649506
Zhao, H., Chen, H., & Yang, F. (2024). Explainability for Large Language Models: A Survey. https://doi.org/10.1145/3639372
